coredata.dc:title: Dual-emission carbon nanodots as a ratiometric nanosensor for the detection of glucose and glucose oxidase
coredata.dc:creator: Qu, Fei
Guo, Xinyu
Liu, Dongya
Chen, Guang
You, Jinmao
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, utilizing dual-emission carbon nanodots (DECNDs), both single signal and ratiometric detection could be developed for glucose and glucose oxidase (GOx) with high sensitivity and selectivity. Glucose could be efficiently catalyzed by GOx to generate gluconic acid and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2); in the presence of Fe2+, based on Fenton reaction (Fe2+ and H2O2), hydroxyl radicals (OH) could be easily achieved and caused the fluorescence quenching of DECNDs. Under optimum conditions, this fluorescent nanosensor showed excellent sensitivity with the limit of detection (LOD) of glucose 0.03μM and LOD of GOx 1.5×10−5 U/mL. The proposed method was successfully applied to the determination of glucose in real samples with satisfactory recoveries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-emission carbon nanodots
Glucose
Glucose oxidase
Ratiometric detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quenching of DECNDs based on Fenton reaction
Optimizing experimental conditions and detection of H2O2
The principle for detection of glucose and GOx
Detection of glucose and GOx based on DECNDs
Selectivity for glucose and GOx detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Fei Qu and Xinyu Guo contributed equally to this work. This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Youth Fund Project) ( 21405093 ) and the Scientific Research Foundation of Qufu Normal University ( BSQD20130117 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra ((a) blue emitters; (b) yellow emitters) of DECNDs in the absence and presence of H2O2, Fe2+, H2O2 and Fe2+, resepctively. The concentrations of H2O2 and Fe2+ were 75μM and 100μM, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence responses to different concentrations of glucose and corresponding linear ranges (a and b, blue emitters; c and d, yellow emitters). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence spectra of DECNDs in the presence of different concentrations of GOx and corresponding linear ranges (a and b, blue emitters; c and d, yellow emitters). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The selectivity of this assay toward glucose. The concentration of glucose was 30μM, and the concentrations of other interfering substances were 300μM ((a) blue emitters; (b) yellow emitters). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The selectivity of this assay toward GOx. The concentration of GOx was 1.0U/mL. The concentrations of pepsin, trypsin and transferrin were 10U/mL. The concentrations of HAS and BSA were 50μM ((a) blue emitters; (b) yellow emitters). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Comparison of different methods for glucose detection.
Determination of glucose in human plasma and glucose injections.

head.ce:title: Dual-emission carbon nanodots as a ratiometric nanosensor for the detection of glucose and glucose oxidase

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis, Qufu Normal University, Qufu 273165, Shandong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Pharmaceutical Intermediates and Analysis of Natural Medicine, Qufu Normal University, Qufu 273165, Shandong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Pharmaceutical Intermediates and Analysis of Natural Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Northwest Institute of Plateau Biology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Xining 810001, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: The Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis, Qufu Normal University, Qufu 273165, Shandong, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, utilizing dual-emission carbon nanodots (DECNDs), both single signal and ratiometric detection could be developed for glucose and glucose oxidase (GOx) with high sensitivity and selectivity. Glucose could be efficiently catalyzed by GOx to generate gluconic acid and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2); in the presence of Fe2+, based on Fenton reaction (Fe2+ and H2O2), hydroxyl radicals (OH) could be easily achieved and caused the fluorescence quenching of DECNDs. Under optimum conditions, this fluorescent nanosensor showed excellent sensitivity with the limit of detection (LOD) of glucose 0.03μM and LOD of GOx 1.5×10−5 U/mL. The proposed method was successfully applied to the determination of glucose in real samples with satisfactory recoveries.

ce:section.ce:para: Glucose distributed widely in nature as an important monosaccharide. By virtue of glucose acting as a source of energy of the living cells and a metabolic intermediate product in the formation of other complex molecules, the level of glucose in blood was used to diagnose diabetes or hypoglycemia [1]. Therefore, glucose detection was of practical importance in the food and fermentation analysis, environmental monitoring, medical diagnosis [2–4] and so on.

Benefiting from the excellent optical properties of nanomaterials, such as good chemical stability, high quantum yield, and biocompatibility, various nano-probes were constructed for detection of glucose through multiple approaches. (1) Colorimetric detection: Apoferritin paired gold clusters could efficiently catalyze oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) by hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) to produce a blue color reaction, while glucose could be highly catalyzed by glucose oxidase (GOx) to generate gluconic acid and H2O2, so an indirect detection of glucose could be developed with the linear range from 2.0 to 10.0mM [5]. Although this method was simple, convenient and low cost, the sensitivity was not satisfactory. (2) Electrochemical detection: at first, CdTe quantum dots (CdTe QDs) and Au nanoparticles (Au NPs) were integrated on the glassy carbon electrode, and in this reaction, Au NPs acted as an efficient GOx-mimickess to catalytically oxidize glucose to produce H2O2 based on the consumption of dissolved oxygen, resulting in a quenching effect on the electrochemiluminescence [6]. (3) Fluorescence detection: a 3D porous carbon foam, consisting of Fe and N-incorporated carbon nanotubes, could catalyze H2O2 to generate hydroxyl radicals (OH), which could oxidized the non-fluorescence of terephthalic acid to the fluorescent hydroxyterephthalate [7]. The fluorescence approach exhibited simplicity, rapid response and high specificity, but these fluorescent nanoprobes for glucose were mainly based on single-signal modulation (turn-off or turn-on). However, it was well known that many interference factors would influence the single-signal detection, for instance, probe concentration, illumination intensity, optical path length, or environmental effect in complex samples. Hence, ratiometric fluorescence detection attracted special interest due to anti-interference ability and reliability [8–13] by calculating the ratio of fluorescence intensities at two different wavelengths.

On the other hand, as an emerging luminescent material, fluorescent carbon nanodots (CNDs) were a new platform for designing multifunctional sensors. In comparison with semiconductor quantum dots and fluorescent dye molecules, CNDs showed such advantages as low toxicity, low cost, and environment friendly. For example, water-soluble CNDs were prepared by pyrolysis of ethylenediamine-tetraacetic acid (EDTA) salts, and these CNDs with no further chemical modification could detect Hg2+ and biothiols; [14] through hydrothermal process, aloe as a carbon source could also be used to synthesize CNDs, which exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity toward tartrazine [15]. More importantly, the development of CNDs eliminated the need to use quantum dots, organic dyes and organic solvents, and this was more environment-friendly.

In previous report, we developed a green and simple synthesis of dual-emission carbon nanodots (DECNDs), which contained two types of emitters, ascorbic acid (AA) capped on the blue emitters (λex=315nm, λem=386nm) and alcohol molecules capped on the yellow emitters (λex=365nm, λem=530nm) [16]. Herein, glucose was catalyzed by GOx to produce H2O2, and through Fenton reaction between ferrous iron (Fe2+) and H2O2, the reactive OH could effectively quench the fluorescence of these two emitters. By virtue of the unique dual-emission property, both of single emission and ratiometric dual emissions could be used to develop standard calibrations of glucose and GOx. This fluorescent nanosensor showed highly sensitivity with the limit of detection (LOD) of glucose 0.03μM and LOD of GOx 1.5×10−5 U/mL, which were better than the reported literature [5–7,17]. Moreover, it also exhibited high selectivity owing to the high specificity of the enzymatic oxidation, and this developed DECNDs ratiometric sensor was successfully applied to the determination of glucose in glucose injection and human plasma with good accuracy and precision.

Ascorbic acid (AA), ethylene glycol, glucose, glucose oxidase (GOx), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO), lactic acid, KCl, sodium citrate, fructose, lactose, four butyl phosphonium hydroxide (TBPH), NaClO, glutathione, KO2, pepsin, trypsin, human serum albumin (HSA), transferrin, bovine serum albumin (BSA), HCl, acetonitrile, CH3COOH, CH3COONa, (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2·6H2O, Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4 and H2O2 were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Glucose injections (0.25M) were purchased from local pharmacy. HCl-CH3COONa buffers (1M, pH=3.09) were prepared by mixing 1M HCl and 1M CH3COONa according to suitable proportion. Phosphate Buffer (PB) buffers (0.2M, pH=7) were prepared by mixing 0.2M Na2HPO4 and 0.3M NaH2PO4 according to suitable proportion. All the solutions were prepared using ultrapure water produced with a Millipore-Q water system.

The pH values of solutions were measured using a pH meter (Mettler Toledo FE20, Switzerland). The fluorescence spectra were recorded with a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption measurements were carried out using a Cary 300 Bio UV–vis spectrophotometer.

In our previous work, the synthetic method of DECNDs was reported [16]. In a typical procedure, 0.8g AA was dissolved in ethylene glycol-water binary reaction media. The total volume of the mixture was 20mL and the volume fraction of ethylene glycol was 50%. Subsequently, under the vigorous stirring, the mixture was heated at 160°C in a constant temperature drying oven for 70min. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged for 10min at 12,000rpm. Finally, a clear yellow aqueous dispersion containing DECNDs was gained, and these CNDs were stored at ambient environment.

A typical H2O2 detection procedure was conducted as follows. Briefly, the as-prepared DECNDs solutions were diluted 100 times before detection (10μL/mL). On the basis of the different fluorescence intensities, the probe concentration was different for these two emitters. For blue emitters, 30μL DECNDs (10μL/mL) were added to different volumes of H2O2 (1mM), respectively, and these mixtures were diluted to 700μL with water. Subsequently, the pH values of these solutions were adjusted to 3.0 with 200μL of HCl-CH3COONa buffers (1M, pH=3.09). Then, 100μL Fe2+ solutions (1mM) were added to the above mixtures, which were stirred vigorously and incubated for 5min at ambient temperature. Eventually, the fluorescence spectra of the mixtures were recorded by a spectrofluorophotometer. The fluorescence intensity decreased linearly with the increase of the concentration of H2O2, and the quenching rate was expressed as (F0-F)/F0, in which F0 and F represented the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of H2O2, respectively.

For yellow emitters, 150μL DECNDs (10μL/mL) were added to different volumes of H2O2 (1mM), respectively, and these mixtures were diluted to 750μL with water. Subsequently, the pH values of these solutions were adjusted to 3.0 with 200μL of HCl-CH3COONa buffers (1M, pH=3.09). Then, 50μL Fe2+ solutions (1mM) were added to the above mixtures, which were stirred vigorously. The fluorescence spectra were recorded after reaction for 5min at room temperature. Subsequently, a working curve was established by plotting the emission ratios (F410/F530) vs. H2O2 concentrations.

The procedure for glucose detection was described as follows. Firstly, 20μL GOx (50U/mL) and different volumes of glucose (1mM) were added into 100μL phosphate buffer (PB) solution (pH 7.0). The mixtures obtained were kept in room temperature for 60min before adjusting the pH of solution to 3.0 with 200μL HCl (0.1M). Then, the as-prepared DECNDs solutions were diluted 100 times before detection (10μL/mL). For blue emitters, 30μL DECNDs (10μL/mL) were added to the obtained mixtures, respectively, and these solutions were diluted to 900μL with water. Finally, 100μL Fe2+ solutions (1mM) were added, respectively, to the above solutions, which were stirred vigorously and incubated for 5min at ambient temperature. The emission spectra were recorded by a spectrofluorophotometer. The fluorescence intensity decreases linearly with the increase of the concentration of glucose, and the quenching rate was expressed as (F0 −F)/F0, in which F0 and F represented the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of glucose, respectively.

For yellow emitters, 150μL DECNDs (10μL/mL) were added to the obtained mixtures and these solutions were diluted to 950μL with water. Then, 50μL Fe2+ solutions (1mM) were added to the above mixtures, which were stirred vigorously. The emission spectra were recorded after reaction for 5min at room temperature. Finally, a linear equation could be developed to describe the relationship between the emission ratios (F410/F530) and glucose concentrations.

The different volumes of GOx and 30μL glucose (1mM) were added into 100μL PB solution (pH 7.0). These mixtures obtained were kept for 60min before adjusting the pH to 3.0 with 200μL HCl (0.1M). The following experiments were same as above mentioned in part 2.5 “Fluorescence detection of glucose”.

In this assay, we detected glucose in human plasma and glucose injections. Detection of glucose in human plasma: the fresh human blood samples were supplied by the lab volunteers and all the blood samples were obtained through venipuncture. Firstly, 3mL blood samples were kept at 37°C in a water bath for about 1h and the blood coagulation was preconditioned. In order to eliminate the possible interference of proteins in the samples without blood clots, the samples without blood clots were deproteinized by adding acetonitrile (the volume of acetonitrile and plasma was 2:1), and then the mixtures were centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 20min to remove the part of deposition. Subsequently, the deproteinized solutions were kept in a water bath (65°C) for about 1h to evaporate acetonitrile. Eventually, a moderate volume of pretreatment blood samples were added into DECNDs system for fluorescence detection of glucose. Besides, different concentrations of glucose standards, 20μL GOx (50U/mL) and diluted DECNDs (30μL DECNDs for blue emitters, 150μL DECNDs for yellow emitters) were spiked to the pretreatment blood samples, and the fluorescence of the solutions were recorded.

Detection of glucose in glucose injections: a bottle of glucose injection (containing glucose 0.25M), was transferred into a beaker and the solution was diluted 1000 times to ensure the concentration of glucose in the linear range. Afterwards, 20μL of the diluted sample was used for fluorescence detection of glucose. Finally, different concentrations of glucose standards, 20μL GOx (50U/mL) and diluted DECNDs (30μL DECNDs for blue emitters, 150μL DECNDs for yellow emitters) were spiked to 20μL pretreatment glucose samples to further analyze.

Validation of the peroxidase-like activity of DECNDs: Three samples were prepared: (1) 150μL DECNDs (10μL/mL) and 20μL TMB (30mM) were mixed with 100μL H2O2 (1mM); (2) 20μL TMB (30mM) and 100μL Fe2+ solutions (1mM) were mixed with 100μL H2O2 (1mM); (3) 150μL DECNDs (10μL/mL), 20μL TMB (30mM) and 100μL H2O2 (1mM) were mixed with 100μL Fe2+ solutions (1mM). Then, 200μL CH3COOH-CH3COONa buffer solution (1M, pH=3.09) was added into these mixtures, respectively. Subsequently, these solutions were diluted to 1.0mL with water and then stirred thoroughly. Eventually, these mixtures were incubated in room temperature for 5min and then were photographed.

Validation of the quenching effect of OH on DECNDs: Briefly, 150μL DECNDs (10μL/mL), 20μL TMB (30mM) and 100μL Fe2+ solutions (1mM) were mixed with various volumes of H2O2 (1mM), and then 200μL CH3COOH-CH3COONa buffer solution (1M, pH=3.09) was added into these mixtures, respectively. Subsequently, these solutions were diluted to 1.0mL with water and then stirred thoroughly. Eventually, these mixtures were incubated in room temperature for 5min and then were photographed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of DECNDs based on Fenton reaction
ce:section.ce:para: In our previous literature, the synthesis and characterization of the DECNDs were studied in detail [16]. The DECNDs were well dispersed and showed an approximately spherical shape; the diameters of them ranged from 1 to 3nm with an average diameter of about 1.81nm; oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl and carbonyl groups on the surfaces of the two emitters ensured the good water solubility. The fluorescence spectra indicated that the DECNDs contained two different emitters (Fig. S1): The blue emitters showed the maximum emission at 386nm under the excitation of 315nm; the yellow emitters exhibited a maximum emission around 530nm when excited at 365nm. Correspondingly, the UV–vis absorption spectra displayed that the DECNDs had a strong peak at 256nm and a weak absorption at 370nm. The dual emissions of CNDs were actually ascribed to two types of CNDs owing to the different modified ligands on their surfaces, such as AA capped on the blue emitters (λex=315nm, λem=386nm) and alcohol molecules capped on the yellow emitters (λex=365nm, λem=530nm) [16].

According to Fig. 1 , in presence of H2O2 or Fe2+, the fluorescence of these two emitters changed inconspicuously, suggesting that there was no interaction between DECNDs and H2O2 or DECNDs and Fe2+; nevertheless, when H2O2 and Fe2+ were added simultaneously to the DECNDs solution, a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity could be observed. The reason was that OH, which was produced by Fenton reaction (Fe2+ +H2O2 →Fe3+ +OH+OH−) [18], caused the quenching of two emissions of DECNDs. To date, a lot of literature reported that OH could directly quench the fluorescence of nanomaterials, such as CNDs [19–22], CdTe quantum dots [23], gold nanoparticles [24,25], and graphene oxide [26]. Hence, OH, as an important active oxygen-containing species with ultrahigh oxidation potential, could destroy the chemical structure of DECNDs leading to fluorescence center destruction and fluorescence quenching [22].

On the other hand, Qu’s group reported a variety of nanoscale materials that possessed unique enzyme-mimic catalytic activities, such as carbon-based nanomaterials, metal-based nanomaterials, and metal oxide-based nanomaterials [27]. These nanoscaled materials with peroxidase-like activity could catalytically oxidize TMB by H2O2 to produce an obvious color change. However, DECNDs used in this experiment did not have intrinsic peroxidase-like activity. As seen in Fig. S2, DECNDs could not directly catalyze the oxidation of TMB by H2O2 to produce the typical blue color without Fe2+; meanwhile, under UV light, the fluorescence intensity of DECNDs was not affected. However, upon the addition of Fe2+ to TMB (the peroxidase substrate) in the presence of H2O2, a blue color change could be observed, because OH, which was produced by the reaction between Fe2+ and H2O2, catalyzed the oxidation of TMB to produce the blue color reaction; this color change could also be observed in the mixture containing Fe2+, H2O2, TMB, and DECNDs, and in the same time, the fluorescence of DECNDs quenched due to OH. With the concentration of H2O2 increasing, the blue color deepened under visual light (Fig. S3a); correspondingly, under UV light, the fluorescence intensity of DECNDs gradually reduced (Fig. S3b). The color variation of TMB further indicated that OH from Fenton reaction led to the fluorescence quenching of DECNDs.

Besides, in our previous study [28], Fe3+ could also directly decrease the blue emission rather than the yellow one due to a redox reaction based on the oxidizing property of Fe3+ and reducing feature of AA capped on the blue emitters; in this process, electrons in the excited state of blue emitters could transfer to the unfilled orbital of Fe3+ and led to non-radiative electron/hole recombination, which resulted in fluorescence quenching [29]. Similar electron transfer between Fe3+ and CNDs was reported by previous literature [30]. However, because of the alcohol molecules capped on the yellow emitters, Fe3+ could not quench the fluorescence of yellow emitters (Fig. S4). Therefore, Fe3+ produced in Fenton reaction was another reason for the fluorescence quenching of blue emitters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing experimental conditions and detection of H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: In this assay, for the blue emitters, the single emission at 386nm was used to detect H2O2; for the yellow emitters excited at 365nm, due to the appearance of the two emissions, the ratiometric detection (F410/F530) was developed. Several experimental parameters were systematically investigated to establish optimal conditions, including the concentration of probe, the concentration of Fe2+, the pH value, the temperature and the reaction time (Fig. S5–S9). Based on the different fluorescent properties of the two emitters, the suitable concentrations of DECNDs were 0.3μL/mL for blue emitters and 1.5μL/mL for yellow emitters, respectively (Fig. S5). The appropriate concentrations of Fe2+ were 100μM for blue emitters and 50μM for yellow emitters, respectively (Fig. S6). In the pH optimization, the stable fluorescence intensity for DECNDs could be achieved over the pH range 3–8 [16] and the optimal pH value of the Fenton reaction was 3 [31–33], so pH 3 was selected (Fig. S7). Furthermore, the effect of incubation temperature on the reaction was investigated in Fig. S8, and at first the fluorescence intensities of DECNDs were not affected by the temperature when they were excited at 315 or 365nm; subsequently, when H2O2 and Fe2+ were added simultaneously to DECNDs, a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity could be observed due to the formation of OH; however, the degree of fluorescence quenching was also not affected by the temperature. In order to facilitate the experimental operation, the room temperature was chosen as the reaction temperature. In addition, the reaction could complete within 5min (Fig. S9), providing a rapid determination of H2O2.

Under the optimized conditions, both single signal and ratiometric detection could be utilized to detect H2O2. At the excitation of 315nm, the linear range of H2O2 was from 0.5 to 100μM with LOD of 0.21μM (Fig. S10a and b). When excited at 365nm, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F410/F530) gradually decreased with increasing the concentrations of H2O2 and a good linearity was obtained in the range from 0.5 to 75μM with LOD 0.17μM (Fig. S10c and d). The sensitivity of H2O2 detection could be compared favorably with that of previous reports [34–38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The principle for detection of glucose and GOx
ce:section.ce:para: H2O2 was the main product of GOx catalyzed reaction of glucose and oxygen (glucose+O2 +H2O→GOx→gluconic acid+H2O2). However, in absence of Fe2+, the glucose and GOx could not affect the fluorescence of DECNDs (Fig. S11), suggesting DECNDs were not sensitive to glucose and GOx. While in presence of Fe2+, based on Fenton reaction, OH could be easily achieved and caused the fluorescence quenching of DECNDs. The amount of OH was proportional to the concentration of glucose or the concentration of GOx, so an indirect strategy for detecting glucose and GOx could be developed.

The optimal pH reported for GOx catalyzed reaction was usually in the range from 6.5 to 7.5 [39–42]. However, as mentioned above, the optimum of pH value in Fenton reaction was 3 [31–33]. Hence, the detection of glucose and GOx was divided to two steps. (1) Enzyme reaction: the reaction of glucose and GOx was conducted in PB buffer solution (pH 7.0) for 60min to generate H2O2; (2) Fenton reaction: the mixture mentioned above was adjusted to 3.0 with 0.1M HCl; then Fe2+ and DECNDs were added.

Moreover, temperature was an important factor of enzyme catalytic reaction. In Fig. S12, the fluorescence quenching of DECNDs was not affected when the temperature of the enzyme catalyzed reaction was from 20 to 40°C. As the temperature continued to increase, the extent of fluorescence quenching gradually reduced. Hence, temperature could influence the catalytic reaction and GOx activity. In this work, the room temperature was selected in the enzyme catalytic reaction.

Additionally, it should be mentioned that phosphate would cause the fluorescence variations of DECNDs (Fig. S13). When excited at 315nm, the emission wavelength exhibited slight red shift and the fluorescence intensity did not decline or increase. When excited at 365nm, the emission wavelengths did not change and both of the dual emissions increased compared with DECNDs in absence of phosphate. The reason might be ascribed to the interaction between phosphate and the surface groups of DECNDs [43]. However, this variation would not affect the response of DECNDs to glucose and GOx.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glucose and GOx based on DECNDs
ce:section.ce:para: According to Fig. 3 , at the excitation of 315nm (Fig. 3a and b), the fluorescence intensity reduced gradually with the enhancement of GOx concentrations, and a good linear range of GOx was observed between 5×10−5 and 0.1U/mL with LOD as low as 1.5×10−5 U/mL. At the excitation of 365nm (Fig. 3c and d), with increasing GOx concentrations, the fluorescence band at 410nm stayed almost unchanged while that at 530nm was gradually quenched. The fluorescence ratios (F410/F530) decreased linearly with increasing GOx concentrations, and a good linear correlation was obtained in the range from 1×10−4 to 2.5U/mL with LOD 3.5×10−5 U/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity for glucose and GOx detection
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of DECNDs toward glucose was investigated. Under the optimal condition, the influence of some species including lactic acid, ascorbic acid, KCl, sodium citrate, fructose, lactose, four butyl phosphonium hydroxide (TBPH), NaClO, glutathione and KO2 were tested (Fig. 4 ). Compared with the response to 30μM glucose, 10 times of the above-mentioned species did not interfere with the determination of glucose. The results clearly demonstrated an excellent selectivity of the sensing system for glucose.

To further explore the selectivity of the assay toward GOx, five kinds of potentially interfering substances including pepsin, trypsin, human serum albumin (HSA), transferrin, and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were selected. As shown in Fig. 5 , there was no apparent variation in fluorescence intensity observed in presence of these interferences.

To assess the feasibility of the sensing system, glucose concentrations in human plasma samples and glucose injections were examined. As shown in Table 2 , the found amount was the glucose concentrations in the real samples without addition of standard, while the found values were the total concentrations of glucose with addition of standard. The recoveries of samples were found to be in the range from 95 to 102% with the relative standard deviation (RSD) within 5%, illustrating that this proposed method could be applied to the detection of glucose in real samples.

In summary, DECNDs were firstly used as a fluorescence sensing platform for the detection of glucose and GOx. Glucose could be highly catalyzed by GOx to generate gluconic acid and H2O2; when adding Fe2+, based on Fenton reaction, OH could be easily achieved and produced the fluorescence quenching of DECNDs. Most importantly, both of single emission and ratiometric dual emissions could be used to detect glucose and GOx, and the multi-detection strategies enhanced the accuracy, reliability, and anti-interference of the sensing system. By virtue of the excellent sensitivity and selectivity, this proposed method was used for detection of glucose in plasma samples and glucose injections, and satisfactory recoveries were obtained. Therefore, the simple, eco-friendly, and cost efficient fluorescent nanosensor offered a great promising approach for sensitive detection glucose and GOx in the medicine and clinical diagnosis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Fei Qu and Xinyu Guo contributed equally to this work.
This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Youth Fund Project) (21405093) and the Scientific Research Foundation of Qufu Normal University (BSQD20130117).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.04.085.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel signal-off electrochemiluminescence biosensor for the determination of glucose based on double nanoparticles
Development of a disposable glucose biosensor using electroless-plated Au/Ni/copper low electrical resistance electrodes
Home blood glucose biosensors: a commercial perspective
Factors determining the appearance of glucose in upper and lower respiratory tract secretions
Peroxidase-like activity of apoferritin paired gold clusters for glucose detection
A novel signal-off electrochemiluminescence biosensor for the determination of glucose based on double nanoparticles
Three-dimensional Fe-and N-incorporated carbon structures as peroxidase mimics for fluorescence detection of hydrogen peroxide and glucose
Ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor based on water soluble carbon nanodots with multiple sensing capacities
ICT-not-quenching near infrared ratiometric fluorescent detection of picric acid in aqueous media
The quinoline derivative of ratiometric and sensitive fluorescent zinc probe based on deprotonation
An ICT-based ratiometric probe for hydrazine and its application in live cells
Visualizing ascorbate-triggered release of labile copper within living cells using a ratiometric fluorescent sensor
A pH sensitive ratiometric fluorophore and its application for monitoring the intracellular and extracellular pHs simultaneously
Carbon nanodots as fluorescence probes for rapid, sensitive, and label-free detection of Hg2+ and biothiols in complex matrices
Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots for selective detection of tartrazine in food samples
Generalized one-pot strategy enabling different surface functionalizations of carbon nanodots to produce dual emissions in alcohol-water binary systems
Simultaneous detection of hydrogen peroxide and glucose in human serum with upconversion luminescence
An eco-friendly, simple, and sensitive fluorescence biosensor for the detection of choline and acetylcholine based on C-dots and the Fenton reaction
A sensitive biosensor for the fluorescence detection of the acetylcholinesterase reaction system based on carbon dots
Fluorescence turn-off detection of hydrogen peroxide and glucose directly using carbon nanodots as probes
Investigation into the fluorescence quenching behaviors and applications of carbon dots
CdTe quantum dots (QDs) based kinetic discrimination of Fe2+ and Fe3+, and CdTe QDs-fenton hybrid system for sensitive photoluminescent detection of Fe2+
A visual detection of hydrogen peroxide on the basis of Fenton reaction with gold nanoparticles
Label-free colorimetric sensing of ascorbic acid based on Fenton reaction with unmodified gold nanoparticle probes and multiple molecular logic gates
A simple and facile strategy based on Fenton-induced DNA cleavage for fluorescent turn-on detection of hydroxyl radicals and Fe2+
Catalytically active nanomaterials: a promising candidate for artificial enzymes
Differentiation of multi-metal ions based on fluorescent dual-emission carbon nanodots
Preparation of highly photoluminescent sulfur-doped carbon dots for Fe(III) detection
Ionic liquids as precursors for highly luminescent, surface-different nitrogen-doped carbon dots used for label-free detection of Cu2+/Fe3+ and cell imaging
Role of goethite dissolution in the oxidation of 2-chlorophenol with hydrogen peroxide
Humic acid modified Fenton reagent for enhancement of the working pH range
Advanced oxidation processes for organic contaminant destruction based on the Fenton reaction and related chemistry
Development of Pt/TiO2 nanohybrids-modified SWCNT electrode for sensitive hydrogen peroxide detection
Polyethyleneimine-capped silver nanoclusters as a fluorescence probe for sensitive detection of hydrogen peroxide and glucose
Reusable sensor based on high magnetization carboxyl-modified graphene oxide with intrinsic hydrogen peroxide catalytic activity for hydrogen peroxide and glucose detection
Glutathione-capped CdTe quantum dots for the sensitive detection of glucose
Controllable synthesis of silver nanodendrites on copper rod and its application to hydrogen peroxide and glucose detection
Studies on glucose biosensors based on nonmediated and mediated electrochemical oxidation of reduced glucose oxidase encapsulated within organically modified sol-gel glasses
Glucose sensor for flow injection analysis of serum glucose based on immobilization of glucose oxidase in titania sol-gel membrane
Organically modified sol-gel/chitosan composite based glucose biosensor
Synthesis, characterization, and immobilization of Prussian blue-modified Au nanoparticles: application to electrocatalyticreduction of H2O2
Insight into the enhanced selectivity of phosphate-modified annealed nanodiamond for oxidative dehydrogenation reactions
Carbon nanodots as peroxidase mimetics and their applications to glucose detection
Graphene oxide: intrinsic peroxidase catalytic activity and its application to glucose detection
Copper nanoclusters as peroxidase mimetics and their applications to H2O2 and glucose detection
Colorimetric detection of hydrogen peroxide and glucose using the magnetic mesoporous silica nanoparticles
Controllable synthesis of silver nanodendrites on copper rod and its application to hydrogen peroxide and glucose detection
Graphene quantum dots as a new substrate for immobilization and direct electrochemistry of glucose oxidase: application to sensitive glucose determination
Iron selenide thin film: peroxidase-like behavior, glucose detection and amperometric sensing of hydrogen peroxide
Fluorescent blood glucose monitor by hemin-functionalized graphene quantum dots based sensing system
Label-free Si quantum dots as photoluminescence probes for glucose detection

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Fei Qu is now working in Qufu Normal University, Shandong Province, China. She obtained her PhD degree in analytical chemistry from Southwest University in 2013. Her current research interests include nanomaterial, fluorescence and sensors.
Xinyu Guo is an undergraduate in Qufu Normal University and major in analytical chemistry.
Dongya Liu received BSc degree in chemistry from Jining University in 2013. Now he is a postgraduate in Qufu Normal University. His research lied in carbon nanomaterials.

Guang Chen is a professor of analytical chemistry at Qufu Normal University. He obtained his PhD degree at Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2012. His current research interests are biosensors, chemosensors, fluorescence and environmental analysis and monitoring.

Jinmao You is a professor of analytical chemistry at Qufu Normal University and Chinese Academy of Sciences. He has published over 300 papers and 50 patents and his current research interests are biosensors, HPLC, fluorescence, and analysis of natural products.
